coredata.dc:description: Although chemodynamic therapy (CDT) has proven to be a promising anti-tumor strategy, its efficacy is limited by the insufficient supply of H2O2 in tumor tissues. To solve the problem of insufficient H2O2, in this paper, a novel double-enzyme cascade nanoreactor hyaluronic-cinnamaldehyde Schiff base@glucose oxidase (GOx)/copper doped carbon dot (abbreviation HCFCTG), which constructed by co-assembly of copper doped carbon dot (CuFACDs-TPP), glucose oxidase (GOx) and hyaluronic-cinnamaldehyde Schiff base (HA-CA) was designed for the first time. The HCFCTG released GOx and CuFACDs-TPP under pH stimulation. GOx continues to supply H2O2 to CDT by consuming glucose, while cutting off the supply of nutrients to starve cancer cells to death (ST), ultimately amplifying the therapeutic effect of CDT. CuFACDs-TPP precisely anchors mitochondria to destroy mitochondria and induce apoptosis, while copper ions consume glutathione to amplify reactive oxygen species (ROS) levels. Self‑oxygenation of HCFCTG by Fenton-like reaction down-regulates hypoxia-inducible factor (HIF-1α) to consolidate CDT effect. The 808 nm laser activates the photothermal effect enhances CDT. In vitro and in vivo experiments proved that HCFCTG has good biocompatibility and excellent CDT effect. HCFCTG overcomes the problem of insufficient H2O2 in the CDT process.

head.ce:title: Glucose oxidase/copper‑carbon dots/hyaluronic acid self-assembly for self-supply hydrogen peroxide in a double-enzyme cascade to enhance anti-tumor therapy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Although chemodynamic therapy (CDT) has proven to be a promising anti-tumor strategy, its efficacy is limited by the insufficient supply of H2O2 in tumor tissues. To solve the problem of insufficient H2O2, in this paper, a novel double-enzyme cascade nanoreactor hyaluronic-cinnamaldehyde Schiff base@glucose oxidase (GOx)/copper doped carbon dot (abbreviation HCFCTG), which constructed by co-assembly of copper doped carbon dot (CuFACDs-TPP), glucose oxidase (GOx) and hyaluronic-cinnamaldehyde Schiff base (HA-CA) was designed for the first time. The HCFCTG released GOx and CuFACDs-TPP under pH stimulation. GOx continues to supply H2O2 to CDT by consuming glucose, while cutting off the supply of nutrients to starve cancer cells to death (ST), ultimately amplifying the therapeutic effect of CDT. CuFACDs-TPP precisely anchors mitochondria to destroy mitochondria and induce apoptosis, while copper ions consume glutathione to amplify reactive oxygen species (ROS) levels. Self‑oxygenation of HCFCTG by Fenton-like reaction down-regulates hypoxia-inducible factor (HIF-1α) to consolidate CDT effect. The 808 nm laser activates the photothermal effect enhances CDT. In vitro and in vivo experiments proved that HCFCTG has good biocompatibility and excellent CDT effect. HCFCTG overcomes the problem of insufficient H2O2 in the CDT process.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Chemodynamic therapy (CDT), as a non-invasive and specific treatment mode, has attracted wide attention due to its advantages of high selectivity, independent of oxygen and exogenous stimulation [1]. Abnormal tumor metabolism, redox dyshomeostasis, weak acidity of tumor microenvironment (TME) and high concentration of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) provide suitable conditions for CDT based on Fenton Type reaction.

In recent years, various transition metal ions such as Fe, Co, Cu, Ce, and Pt have been used to catalyze H2O2 to produce hydroxyl radicals (⋅OH) at tumor sites for CDT [2–5]. CDT has great potential in cancer treatment, but the therapeutic effect is still limited due to insufficient H2O2 [6,7]. The high expression of GSH in the tumor microenvironment consumes reactive oxygen species to maintain redox homeostasis, thus inhibiting oxidative stress and seriously reducing the CDT effect [8–10]. The pH (5.5–6.9) of TME and the low Fenton reaction rate (k = 63–76 M−1 s−1) are also the limiting factors for CDT [11,12], and it needs a pH in the 2–4 range. Therefore, increasing H2O2 amount of the TME, decreasing pH values and eliminating GSH become the key strategies for CDT. So far, a large number of direct and indirect methods have been developed to deliver H2O2 from the outside to the TME [13–15], for example, in nanocarriers of polymers that co-deliver H2O2 and Fe3O4 nanoparticles [16]. In addition to direct delivery of H2O2, metal peroxide nanomaterials also provide H2O2 in the tumor region through a hydrolysis reaction [17]. Although these strategies increase H2O2 quantity to a certain extent, they cannot form a sustained H2O2 supply, nor can decrease pH value.

Natural glucose oxidase (GOx) specifically catalyzes β-D glucose to produce gluconic acid and H2O2 with high efficiency [18]. In the tumor cells, GOx completely oxidizes glucose, thus cutting off the energy supply, causing the tumor cells to starve and die (Starvation therapy, ST), and can locally decrease pH values [19]. However, the reactions catalyzed by GOx are highly dependent on O2 amount, and unfortunately the TME is hypoxia, so there is a serious barrier to GOx terminating the enzyme reaction due to oxygen deficiency in these current strategies. Fortunately, oxygen production is one of the characteristics of Fenton reactions [20,21], and thermal effects can enhance the Fenton reaction [22,23]. Based on Fenton-like reaction, Cu2+ has peroxidase-like properties and can catalyze the decomposition of H2O2 under weakly acidic or neutral conditions, and the reaction rate (k = 1 × 104 m−1 s−1) is much higher than that of Fe2+ [24], and Cu2+ is a good GSH consumer.

Carbon dots (CDs) are emerging carbon-based nanomaterials with excellent biocompatibility and optical activity [25–30]. The photothermal conversion function of CDs in the near infrared region and the fluorescence imaging function under the excitation of visible light indicate its application prospect in the field of integrated diagnosis and treatment [31–36]. In particular, the light stability of carbon dots is excellent [37,38]. Hyaluronic acid (HA) is widely found in humans and other vertebrates and is involved in many biological functions [39,40]. CD44 is highly expressed in many cancers of epithelial origin, and the interaction between HA and CD44 is considered to be a key marker of tumor invasion and metastasis. Using the interaction between HA and CD44, a variety of HA-constructed tumor-targeting nanodrug delivery systems have been developed [41,42].

Based on the above analysis, we proposed a strategy to enhance the efficiency of the double-enzyme cascade reaction with photothermal assistance. The previous strategy ignored the supply of oxygen, resulting in the failure of GOx to continuously supply H2O2 for CDT, nor can it down-regulate pH to enhance Fenton reaction, while our proposed strategy based on the cascade reaction of peroxide-like nanozymes and glucose oxidase can continuously supply O2 and H2O2, and it down-regulate pH. Based on this strategy, multifunctional co-assembled nanoparticles (HCFCTG) which composed of hyaluronic acid-cinnamylaldehyde (HA-CA) Schiff base, GOx and triphenylphosphine (TPP) modified copper folate carbon dots (CuFACDs-TPP) is prepared. TPP was a mitochondrial targeting molecule [43]. Due to the presence of Cu2+/Cu+ redox pairs and GOx, HCFCTG had simulated peroxidase activity and glucose oxidase activity, continuously catalyzed the decomposition of glucose to produce gluconic acid and H2O2, continuously supplying H2O2 to CDT, while decreasing pH value further enhanced Fenton-Like reaction. HCFCTG had the following functions:1 continuously self-supplying H2O2, blocking nutrient supply and photothermal effect jointly enhanced CDT; 2 specific responded to TME released CuFACDs-TPP and GOx; 3 CuFACDs-TPP precisely anchored mitochondria to induce apoptosis in cells, copper ions consumed GSH to amplify ROS levels; 4 In situ oxygen supplied alleviates tumor hypoxia. The anti-tumor mechanism of HCFCTG was shown in Scheme 1 .

Hyaluronic acid (95 %; Mw = 10w Da; glucuronic content: 46 %) was purchased from Huaxi Biotech Ltd. (China). Ethylenediamine, FA, copper gluconate, CA, glutathione (GSH), GOx and [Ru(dpp)3]Cl2 (RDPP) were purchased from EnergyChemical (Shanghai,China). 5,5′-Dithiobide (2-nitrobenzoic acid) (DTNB) and 4,6-DAPI were purchased from Aladdin Industrial Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The GSH assay kit was purchased from Nanjing Jiancheng Bioengineering Institute (Najin China). Calcein AM, PI, trizol, CRT antibody, MTT kit, hematoxylin and eosin staining kit (H&E), Hydrogen Peroxide Assay Kit, Mito-Tracker Green and DCFH-DA purchased from Beyotime Biotech Inc. (Shanghai, China). The newborn bovine serum, PBS, RPMI-1640 media, and trypsin−EDTA were purchased from Beijing Solarbio Science & Technology (Beijing, China). The size and morphology of nanoparticles were observed by a Tecnai G2F20S-TWIN transmission electron microscope (FEI, USA). Ultraviolet−visible spectra were recorded with a UV-2600 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy is measured using the ARL QUANT'X EDXRF Spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, Switzerland). XRD spectra were characterized by a D5005 X-ray diffractometer made by Siemens. Raman spectra were recorded using the LabRAM HR800 laser micro confocal Raman spectrometer (HORIBA Jobin Yvon, France). XPS was performed on an ESCALAB 250Xi (Thermo Scientific).

CuFACDs were prepared by hydrothermal method. 40 mg folic acid and 60 mg copper gluconate were dissolved in 40 mL deionized water, ultrasonic for 30 min, and the reaction liquid was transferred to a high-pressure reactor and heated at 180 °C for 8 h. Cooling to room temperature, filtration with 0.22 μ filter membrane, centrifugation of filtrate for 10 min (12,000 rpm).

4 mg CuFACDs was dispersed in 5 mL DMF, mixed with 7.5 mg EDC and 7.5 mg NHS for 6 h, and centrifuged for 10 min. The resulting precipitate was dispersed in 5 mL acetonitrile, 16 mg 2-aminoethyl triphenylphosphine bromide was added, and heated for 1 h. Centrifuge for 10 min (12,000 rpm), wash the precipitate with ethanol and water for 3 times, freeze dry the remaining precipitate and freeze dry.

10 mg CuFACDs-TPP and 2 mg glucose oxidase (GOx) were dispersed in 5 mL ethanol. Dissolve 20 mg hyaluronic acid in 10 mL DMF, added 11.6 mg EDC and 11.6 mg NHS, stirred for 6 h, added 1.7 g ethylenediamine, stirred at room temperature overnight, added 2.7 g cinnamaldehyde, stirred at room temperature for 2 h. The reaction solution was dialysed in deionized water for 72 h (10,000 Da). The solids in the dialysis bag were dispersed into 10 mL of deionized water (adjusted pH to 7.4 with a saturated Na2CO3 solution), CuFACDs-TPP and GOx ethanol dispersions were added, stirred overnight, and then dialyzed with deionized water for 24 h, centrifuged for 10 min (12,000 rpm) to obtain HCFCTG, and the supernatant was retained. The nanoparticles HA@GOx and HA@CuFACDs without GOx or CuFACDs-TPP were prepared by the same method Maked the absorbance standard curves of CuFACDs-TPP and GOx, and calculated the drug loading rate according to the following formula.
where MCuFACDs-TPP is the CuFACDs-TPP mass of the load, MHCFCTG is the mass of HCFCTG, MGOx is the GOx mass of the load.

100 μg mL−1 HCFCTG (pH 7.4, 6.8, 5.5 in PBS) was incubated with different concentrations of glucose (0, 1, 5, 10, 20 mM) for 0–2 h. The hydrodynamic particle size and morphology (TEM) were measured at 0, 1 and 2 h time points, respectively, and the pH value of the solution was measured by pH meter.

The activity of HCFCTG was measured by colorimetric detection. 50 mL HCFCTG (100 μg mL−1) was incubated with different concentrations of glucose (0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 4.0, 8.0 and 16.0 mM) and 5.0 mM TMB for 2 h, and the absorbance at 652 nm was recorded. The pH values at different time points were tested with a pH-meter.

Placed HCFCTG aqueous solution (200 μg mL−1) in the cuvette and light an 808 nm lamp of different powers (0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0 W cm−2) for 15 min. Different concentrations of HCFCTG aqueous solution (0, 100, 200, 300, 400 μ gmL−1) were placed in the cuvette, illuminated with a laser (808 nm, 1.5 W) for 15 min, the temperature was recorded and imaged with an infrared thermal imager.

5 mL HCFCTG (1 mg mL−1) and 10 mmol of H2O2 for 2 h, then take HCFCTG (0,100, 200, 400 μL) into the test tube, add 120 μ L of aqueous glutathione solution (20 μM), then dilute to 4 mL with PBS (pH 5.5, 6.8, 7.4). After mixing, the centrifuge tubes were incubated on a shaker for 1 h, then 60 μL DTNB (20 μ M) was added to continue the incubation for 1 h on the shaker. Absorbance at 325 nm and 412 nm was recorded using a UV–visible spectrophotometer.

The CT26 cell species were grown in 6-well plates at 37 °C for 24 h in an incubator at 5 % CO2. The medium was discarded and washed three times with 1 mL PBS of different nanomaterials (HA@GOx, HA@CuFACDs-TPP, HCFCTG) at different concentrations (0, 50, 200, and 400 μg mL−1) were added and incubated in a 5 % CO2 incubator for 8 h. The material was aspirated and washed three times with PBS.1 mL of ROS probe DCFH-DA (50 μg mL−1) was added to each well for a further 30 min, then observed and photographed under a fluorescence inverted microscope.

1 mL of HCFCTG at different concentrations (0, 50, 100, 200 and 400 μg mL−1) was added to the cell culture bottle with good cell morphology and incubated for 24 h. The medium containing material in the corresponding cell culture bottle and the digested cell suspension were collected. The cells were mixed and the cells were collected by centrifugation. The cells were washed twice with PBS, collected by centrifugation, suspended with 0.5 mL of PBS buffer and crushed by sonication in an ice-cold bath (power 300 W, 3–5 s each, 30 s apart, repeated 3–5 times). The broken homogenate was taken and the GSH content in the cells was determined according to the instructions of the GSH assay kit.

CT26 cells were co-incubated with 1 mL of HA@CuFACDs-TPP, HA@GOX, and HCFCTG (100 μg mL−1) for 2 h, followed by 1 mL trypsin digestion. The digested cells were transferred to a centrifuge tube and the hydrogen peroxide was determined following the operating procedures of the hydrogen peroxide detection kit.

7-week-old, 18–20 g Balb/c female mice were selected as experimental subjects, and CT26 cells were selected for the cell lines. When cells were in the logarithmic growth phase, cells were digested, suspended in PBS (about 1 × 107 mL−1) and injected in the right buttock of mice (100 μ L, about 1 million cells). When the tumor volume reached 100 mm3, HCFCTG was administered through the tail vein at a dose of 10 mg kg−1. It was observed in in vivo fluorescence imager and infrared thermal imager at different time points after administration. Heart, liver, spleen, lung and kidney tumor tissues were obtained according to the peak time of tumor enrichment shown by imaging, and drug enrichment in organs and tumors was observed on a fluorescence imaging instrument.

7-week-old female Balb/c mice were selected as model animals, and CT26 cells were inoculated subcutaneously. When the tumor volume was about 100 mm3, they were randomly divided into 5 groups (5 mice in each group): Saline group, GOx group, CuFACDs-TPP group, GOx + CuFACDs-TPP group, and HCFCTG+Light group. HCFCTG was administered via the tail vein at 10 mg kg−1 on days 0, 5 and 10. Irradiation with 808 nm laser for 10 min (50 mW cm−2) 6 h after administration. Treatment ended on day 14. The body status of the mice was observed every day during treatment, and the volume and weight of the tumors were recorded. Note that in order to avoid burns caused by laser irradiation, intermittent irradiation should be performed at an interval of about 3 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assays of HIF-1α in tumor tissue
ce:section.ce:para: After 14 days of treatment, the tumor tissues from different treatment groups were formed into paraffin sections, followed by deparaffinization, antigen retrieval, fixation, and sealing. According to the kit instructions, the tumor tissues were incubated with primary and secondary antibodies, then stained with immunofluorescence and analyzed using a fluorescence inverted microscope.

All experiments were repeated >3 times. All data were expressed as mean ± standard deviation. One-way ANOVA was performed to compare the statistical analysis by GraphPad Prism 8.0. *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and structural characterization of HCFCTG
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis process of HCFCTG was shown in Scheme 2 . Using FA and copper gluconate as carbon and copper sources, CuFACDs were synthesized in one step by hydrothermal method. The surface carboxyl group of CuFACDs was activated by EDC/NHS and then aminated with 2-aminoethyl triphenylphosphine bromide (TPP) to obtain CuFACDS-TPP. HA was activated by EDC/NHS, then aminated by ethylenediamine, and then condensed with cinnamaldehyde (CA) to obtain hyaluronic acid cinnamaldehyde schiff base (HA-CA). HA-CA, GOx and CuFACDs-TPP were self-assembled to obtain multifunctional nanoparticles HA@CuFACD-TPP@GOx (abbreviated as HCFCTG).

The morphology and size of CuFACDs-TPP and HCFCTG were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Fig. 1a showed that CuFACDs-TPP was uniformly spherical and its size was distributed in the range of 2–5 nm (lower left panel in Fig. 1a). High-resolution transmission microscopy (HRTEM) shown that the lattice spacing of the carbon dots was d = 0.21 nm, corresponding to the graphene (100) crystal surface (top right panel in Fig. 1a) [44]. Fig. 1b showed that HCFCTG was an uneven spherical particle with a particle size distribution between 60 and 150 nm. HRTEM clearly showed the graphene lattice stripes of carbon points (d = 0.21 nm) (Fig. 1b). Raman spectra of both CuFACDs-TPP and HCFCTG (Fig. 1c) showed the same D-peak and G-peak, which proved that the two were successfully assembled together. Fig. 1d was X-Ray Diffraction (XRD), which had strong diffraction peaks at 43.32°, 50.44° and 74.4°, these peaks coincided with the characteristic diffraction peaks of Cu nanocrystals (CuNC, PDF#85-1326). Therefore, the copper element in CuFACDs-TPP was attached to the surface of the carbon dot in the nanocrystalline phase. Therefore, it was proved that CuFACDs-TPP was embedded in HCFCTG. Fig. 1e showed X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of HCFCTG, with electron emission peaks at 284 eV, 397 eV, 533 eV, 875 eV, and 47 eV, respectively. These peaks are attributed to elements C, N, O, Cu and P, respectively, indicating that HCFCTG was composed of these elements, and P elements prove that TPP components exist in it. Fig. 1f shown the XPS of Cu2p. There are relatively strong electron diffraction peaks at 935.8 eV and 957.5 eV, which corresponding to Cu(0) 2p3/2 and Cu(0)2p1/2, respectively, which proved that Cu exists mainly in the 0 valence state, which was consistent with XRD results. There were relatively weak electron diffraction peaks at 932.7 eV and 953.3 eV, corresponding to Cu(II)2p3/2 and Cu(II)2p1/2, respectively. The peaks between 938.3 and 942.7 eV were satellite peaks of Cu(II), indicating that CuFACDs-TPP surface was modified with a small amount of Cu(II) [45]. Fig. 1g was the element mapping, where the C, N, O, P, and Cu elements were shown, with the same results as XPS (Fig. 1e). Fig. 1h showed that the Zeta potential of CuFACDs was −38.14 mV, which changes to +25.52 mV after modification of TPP. This result was related to the positive charge of TPP, which proved that TPP was successfully modified on CuFACDs. The Zeta potential of HCFCTG tends to be negative, which was related to the large amount of carboxyl, hydroxyl and other functional groups contained in HA and GOx. Fig. S1 shown that CuFACDs-TPP had absorption in the 300–900 nm region, and HCFCTG also had absorption in this region (Fig. 1i), which could also proved that CuFACDs-TPP was successfully embedded in HCFCTG. Fig. 1j shown the fluorescence emission spectrum of HCFCTG. Under the excitation of 410 nm light, strong fluorescence emission was emitted at 505 nm, and the base edge of the emission peak extends to the red region, showing the potential fluorescence imaging potential. Fig. 1k shown the Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR). The vibration peaks of benzene ring (1400–1600 cm−1) were found in CuFACDS-TPP, which proved that TPP was successfully modified in CuFACDs. The benzene ring vibration peaks (1400–1600 cm−1) and the CN double bond vibration peak of Schiff base (~1570 cm−1) were found in HCFCTG, which proved that there were CuFACDs-TPP and Schiff base structure in HCFCTG. Fig. 1l was the CuFACDs-TPP release behavior of HCFCTG in PBS at different pH values. It barely released within 12 h in pH 7.4 solution, released 24.6 % in pH 6.8 solution, and released 56.8 % at pH 5.5. With the decrease of pH value, the release rate increased. The results showed that the release rate increased with the increase of acidity, which proved that the Schiff base was destroyed and the co-assembly nanostructure decomposed under acidic conditions, demonstrating the pH response to drug release function. Fig. 1m shown the dynamic process of HCFCTG morphology change in pH 5.5 solution. The morphology of the nanoparticles was complete in pH 7.4 solution at 180 min, and the nanostructures were completely degraded in pH 5.5 solution at 120 min, which was consistent with the release behavior in acidic solution, demonstrating the potential TME responded to drug slowly release function. Fig. 1n shown that the absorbance of HCFCTG in pH 7.4 PBS, normal saline, and medium (RMPI1640 + 10 % FBS) basically remained unchanged (0–7 days), indicating good stability. The loading rate of CuFACDs-TPP and GOx were 31.7 % and 9.2 %, respectively, as calculated from the standard curve.

HCFCTG had absorption in the visible-near-infrared region, so the photothermal conversion ability of 808 nm laser irradiation was investigated. HCFCTG solution with different concentrations was irradiated by 808 nm laser (1.5 W cm−2), and the results were shown in Fig. 2a. The temperature of the solution increased significantly with the increase of concentration and illumination time. After illumination for 10 min, the temperature of the solution with a concentration of 400 μg mL−1 rose to 47 °C, after 15 min of illumination, the temperature rose to 52 °C, while the temperature of the control group (H2O) only rose to 2.5 °C. Fig. 2b shown that when HCFCTG (400 μg mL−1) was irradiated by different light sources, the solution temperature increased with the increase of light radiation intensity, and the photothermal conversion capacity of HCFCTG was positively correlate with the dose and light irradiation intensity. Fig. 2c was the photothermal image of HCFCTG, which clearly shown the change of HCFCTG solution temperature with the illumination time, demonstrating excellent photothermal imaging potential. Fig. 2d was the temperature change of HCFCTG under the control of the light source switch. When the solution was irradiated by light, the temperature rose rapidly, and when the light source was removed after the temperature stable, the solution temperature dropped rapidly to the initial temperature, HCFCTG demonstrated excellent light responded heating effect. Fig. 2e was a linear relationship curve of -lnθ-Time, and the correlation coefficient R2 is 0.991, indicating a good linear relationship between light time and temperature. Fig. 2f shown the cyclic temperature rise curve of HCFCTG. The maximum temperature in each cycle was basically consistent, indicating that HCFCTG had good photothermal stability. According to the reference method [46], the photothermal conversion efficiency of HCFCTG was 28.4 %. GOx is a biological protein, and high temperature can cause it to lose enzymatic activity, so it is important to understand its thermal stability when applying in vivo and in vitro. The residual enzyme activity was measured by holding the liquid and different temperatures for 60 min (the measurement condition of the control enzyme solution was pH 6.8, the temperature was 25 °C, and the enzyme activity was directly measured without holding the temperature), as shown in Fig. S2. The thermal stability of GOx was very good in the range of 25–63 °C, enzyme activity over control group (25 °C) and it decreased significantly when the temperature exceeded 63 °C. The enzyme activity was also above 100 % after treatment at 25–63 °C, which showed that the stability of GOx was very good. The enzyme activity was the highest at 40 °C, which was 167 % of that in control group (25 °C). After treatment at 63 °C for 60 min, 52 % vitality remained. Therefore, in the subsequent in vitro experiments, intermittent irradiation will be adopted to control the temperature not exceeding 45 °C according to the dose dependence shown in Fig. 2a.

GOx catalyzes the oxidation of glucose to produce gluconic acid and H2O2, so the pH gradually decreases and the acidity increases as the reaction proceeds. HCFCTG was a co-assembly composed of HA-CA Schiff base, CuFACDs-TPP and GOx. Under acidic conditions, the Schiff base was destroyed and the nanostructure degraded to release GOx [47]. Therefore, the degradation properties of HCFCTG in an environment rich in glucose were investigated and compared with HA@CuFACDs under the same conditions. HA@CuFACDs-TPP and HCFCTG were immersed in 20 mM glucose solution for 0–2 h. The morphologies were observed by TEM, and the hydrodynamic dimensions were measured. As shown in Fig. 3a, the structure of HCFCTG was gradually destroyed until it was completely degraded, while HA@CuFACDs-TPP was basically unchanged. Fig. 3b shown that HA@CuFACDs-TPP particle size remains almost constant. Fig. 3c shown that the particle size of HCFCTG was about 100 nm at the beginning, and gradually increased with the increase of incubation time, reaching about 510 nm after 1 h and 700 nm after 2 h. This result implied that GOx, by catalyzing the glucose oxidation reaction to produce gluconic acid, reduced the local pH value leading to the breakdown of nanostructures. pH played an important role in the structural dissociation of HCFCTG. In order to support the fact that acidic pH caused the decomposition of HCFCTG nanostructures, we analyzed the pH changes after incubation with different concentrations of glucose solutions. The result shown that with the increase of glucose concentration, pH value gradually decreased, and pH value was inversely proportional to the concentration and incubation time. The downward trend of pH value was opposite to the trend of DLS increase. Thus, it was shown that the acidity destroys the nanostructure. When HCFCTG (400 μg mL−1) was incubated in a 20 mM glucose solution for 20 h, the pH value decreased from 7.4 to 3.2. The fact that this local pH value continues to drop can be explained by the in-situ production of gluconic acid, supporting the fact that glucose was oxidized to produce gluconic acid.

To investigate the catalytic activity of HCFCTG on glucose, we used 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to determine ⋅OH, the end product of the double-enzyme cascade reaction [48]. HCFCTG, 20 mM glucose and TMB were incubated together. As shown in Fig. 3e, an absorption peak appeared at 652 nm, and the solution changed from colorless to blue, indicating that TMB was oxidized to ox-TMB, while HCFCTG alone could not oxidize TMB. It could be proved that HCFCTG catalyzed the oxidation of glucose to H2O2, and then H2O2 started the Fenton-like reaction to produce ⋅OH, and ⋅OH oxidized TMB to ox-TMB. Fig. 3e shown that the presence of glucose promoted the initiation of the enzyme cascade catalytic reaction and enhanced the generation of ROS and the oxidation of TMB. Next, another OH trapping agent MB was used to demonstrate the generation of H2O2 [49]. MB has an absorption peak at 650 nm, which disappeared after degradation by ⋅OH, and the absorbance of MB was inversely proportional to the amount of ⋅OH. Results as shown in Fig. 3f, after co-incubation of HCFCTG (400 μg mL−1), different doses of glucose (0–20 mM) and MB, MB absorbance gradually decreased with the increase of glucose dose, while MB absorbance remained unchanged in the control group, demonstrating that HCFCTG catalyzed the decomposition of glucose to produce H2O2. Then a cascade of Fenton-like reactions occurred to generate ⋅OH, and ⋅OH degraded MB. Using TMB as a probe, the kinetics of the double-enzyme cascade reaction catalyzed by HCFCTG was studied in glucose solutions of different concentrations (Fig. 3g). With the increase of glucose concentration, the reaction rate of TMB oxidation also increased, which led to a continuous increased in the absorption intensity at 652 nm. The double-enzyme cascade reaction mechanism of HCFCTG was shown in Fig. 3h. HCFCTG catalyzed the oxidation of glucose to produce gluconic acid and H2O2 (reaction 1), reducing the local pH value, and the PH-sensitive degradation of HCFCTG released CuFACDs-TPP (reaction 2). CuNC was oxidized to Cu2+ by H2O2 under acidic conditions, and a small amount of reducing agent GSH was added to reduce Cu2+ to Cu+ (reaction 3). Cu+ started Fenton-like reaction (reaction 4) to produce ⋅OH, and ⋅OH oxidized TMB to produce oxi-TMB (reaction 5). OH degradation of MB (reaction 6) (650 nm absorption peak disappeared). The above results confirmed that HCFCTG showed good double-enzyme cascade activity, demonstrating its ability to sustain H2O2 supply and produce high toxicity ⋅OH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ability of HCFCTG to oxidize GSH
ce:section.ce:para: Cu2+ is an excellent GSH consuming agent, which oxidizes GSH to GSSG through REDOX reaction, rendering it non-reductive [50]. Therefore, HCFCTG had the potential to oxidize GSH. DTNB is a GSH probe [51], which has absorption at 325 nm. After being reduced by GSH, the peak disappears and a new peak appears at 412 nm. The absorbance at 325 nm is inversely proportional to the GSH content. The copper element in HCFCTG mainly exists in two valence states, Cu0 and Cu2+, and Cu0 had no ability to oxidize GSH. In order to maximize the ability of HCFCTG to oxidize GSH, we first oxidized Cu0 to Cu2+ with H2O2, and added a certain amount of H2O2 to HCFCTG solution, and reacted at room temperature for 2 h. HCFCTG treated with H2O2 was co-incubated with GSH for 1 h, and DTNB was added to co-incubate for 1 h. The results were shown in Fig. 3. Fig. 3i shown that under pH 5.5, when DTNB was co-incubated with GSH, the absorption peak at 325 nm completely disappeared, while the maximum absorption peak appeared at 412 nm, indicating that DTNB was completely reduced by GSH in the absence of HCFCTG. After the addition of HCFCTG, the absorption peak at 325 nm gradually increased with the increase of dose, and the absorption peak at 412 nm gradually decreased, indicating that the content of GSH gradually decreased, which proved that HCFCTG oxidized GSH and DTNB was retained, demonstrating the excellent GSH oxidizing ability of HCFCTG. Continue to investigate the effect of pH on HCFCTG's ability to oxidize GSH. Fig. 3j shown that under the same conditions, the ability to oxidize GSH gradually increased with the decrease of pH, indicating that acidity was conducive to the oxidation of GSH. Generally, temperature had a great influence on the reoxidation reaction, so the oxidation of GSH by HCFCTG under 808 nm laser irradiation was investigated. Fig. 3k shown that under the same conditions, GSH consumption in the light group was greater, indicating that HCFCTG's ability to oxidize GSH was enhanced with the assistance of photothermal effect. The above experimental results fully proved that the GSH consumption capacity of HCFCTG was dose-dependent, and acidity and heat were favorable to the oxidation of GSH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: HA mediated cellular uptake and mitochondrial targeting
ce:section.ce:para: Before the discussion, we first state that in order to prevent high-temperature protein denaturation, intermittent irradiation was used in all light experiments (except MTT analysis), and the light time was controlled according to the temperature curve (Fig. 2a) so that the temperature does not exceed 45 °C. CT26 cell is a mouse colon cancer cell line, and CD44 protein is highly expressed on its surface [52]. In this study, CT26 cells were used as in vitro experimental model cells. The expression of CD44 on many tumor cell membranes is higher than that of normal cells [53]. The N-terminal of CD44 peptide chain can bind to hyaluronic acid, so CD44 is also regarded as a receptor of hyaluronic acid, which is involved in the uptake and degradation of hyaluronic acid by cells. Therefore, hyaluronic acid modified nanocarriers are preferentially enriched in tumor tissues. CD44 protein was highly expressed on the surface of CT26 cells, so the uptake of HCFCTG by CT26 cells under the mediation of HA was investigated. HCFCTG emits red fluorescence under the excitation of green light source, and the intracellular red fluorescence intensity is proportional to the cell intake. The nucleus was stained with DAPI. First, the uptake of HCFCTG by CD44-positive CT26 cells was investigated. Fig. 4a showed the imaging results under fluorescence microscope after 0–3 h co-incubation of HCFCTG and CD26 cells, showing that a large amount of HCFCTG was taken up by cells, and the amount of HCFCTG entering cells was positively correlated with the incubation time. CT26 cells were treated with HA and then incubated with HCFCTG. As showed in Fig. 4b, only a small amount of HCFCTG entered CD26 cells. These results demonstrated that HCFCTG had a specific aggregation ability on tumor cells under mediated by HA. Fig. 4c was a quantitative fluorescence map of cellular uptake, clearly shown the advantages of HA-mediated cellular uptake. MTT method was used to investigate the toxicity of HCFCTG on normal cells L929. The results were shown in Fig. 4d, and it was found that the cell survival rate was nearly 100 % even at a high dose (400 μg mL−1). We also investigated the cytotoxicity of HCFCTG on human normal liver cells THLE-2 and mouse normal liver cells NCTC1469, and the results show that the survival rate of cells was >95 %, which proved that HCFCTG had no cytotoxicity on normal cells (Fig. S3). TPP is a lipophilic cation, containing one phosphorus atom and three phenyl groups, among which the phenyl group is lipophilic and can interact with the hydrophobic region in the mitochondrial membrane, so that triphenylphosphine was mainly enriched in the mitochondria in the cell [54]. In addition, the mitochondrial membrane potential of normal cells was higher (−140 mV), and the mitochondrial membrane potential of tumor cells was higher (−200 mV) than that of normal cells. Based on the charge principle, electrophilic phosphonium cations could more easily enter the mitochondria of tumor cells through electrostatic interaction, showing mitochondrial targeting [55]. Modification of mitochondria targeted TPP on the surface of nanoparticles can effectively promote the aggregation of nanocallers on mitochondria. HCFCTG degrades in an acidic environment and releases CuFACDs-TPP. The Zeta potential of CuFACDs-TPP was +25.52 mV, preferentially attracted to mitochondria with high membrane potential. Therefore, we investigated the mitochondria-targeted aggregation of CuFACDs-TPP in CT26 cells. CuFACDs-TPP was incubated with CT26 cells and observed under confocal laser microscope. The nuclei were stained with DAPI, the mitochondrial probe Mit Tracker green was green, and CuFACDs-TPP was red. Fig. 4e shown that the red fluorescence of CuFACDs-TPP overlaps with the Mit Tracker green. The ImageJ software was used for two-color overlap processing, and the results showed that the red region of CuFACDs-TPP overlaps highly with the green region of Mit Tracker green, which proved that CuFACDs-TPP had accurate mitochondrial targeting.

The RDPP is the probe to investigate the intracellular oxygen level [56]. Red fluorescence of the RDPP is inversely proportional to the oxygen concentration level. CT26 cells, 1 mL HCFCTG (400 μg mL−1) and 50 mL RDPP were incubated (Fig. 4f), which found that the intracellular red fluorescence was still very weak after 10 h of incubation, indicating a high intracellular oxygen level at this time. Fig. 4g shown (the intracellular RDPP fluorescence quantitative histogram), under high dose (400 μg mL−1) and light conditions, the oxygen level increased 56.25 % compared with the control group. HCFCTG the double enzyme cascade occurs in the cell, and GOx consumes oxygen to oxidation glucose to form H2O2. The resulting H2O2 compensates for the intracellular H2O2 concentration, increased the concentration of H2O2, and promoted the peroxidase-like formation of Cu ions to form O2. The O2 generated compensates O2 for GOx catalyzed oxidation reaction. Due to the rapid Fenton reaction rate of Cu ions, the high intracellular glucose and H2O2 concentration, the peroxidase-like catalytic reaction was fast, the final oxygenation efficiency was higher than the oxygen consumption efficiency, and the final intracellular oxygen level increased until the intracellular glucose was completely exhausted. The dual enzyme cascade of HCFCTG ensured the continuous supply of H2O2 and the continuous production of O2.

HIF-1α is a protein that accumulates when cells are starved of oxygen, and it plays a key role in the cell's response to hypoxia. At normal oxygen levels, HIF-1α is hydroxylated by oxygen-dependent prolyl hydroxylase (PHD) and then degraded by the proteasome. However, under hypoxic conditions, PHD activity is reduced and HIF-1α degradation is inhibited, thereby accumulating and activating the transcription of a series of genes involved in energy metabolism, angiogenesis, tumor cell invasion, etc. Therefore, inhibiting HIF-1α expression by up-regulating oxygen level is an important strategy to prevent cancer cell growth and metastasis. Fig. 4f shown that HCFCTG up-regulates the oxygen level in CT26 cells. We investigated the expression of HIF-1α in cells by immunofluorescence method. As shown in Fig. 4h, HIF-1α expression was significantly down-regulated with the increase of HCFCTG concentration. The down-regulation trend of HIF-1α was contrary to the up-regulation trend of O2 level, which confirmed that HCFCTG down-regulated HIF-1α expression through up-regulation of O2 level. Fig. 4i shown the fluorescence quantitative histogram of HIF-1α. Under high dose (400 μg mL−1) and light conditions, the expression of HIF-1α was down-regulated by 67 %. CT26 cells were co-incubated with HCFCTG, and the oxygen level in the cells was regulated by two factors, namely, the GOx oxygenase reaction process and the Cu ion enzyme-like reaction oxygen production process. Cu ion enzyme-like reaction provided substrate (O2) for GOx enzyme reaction, while GOx enzyme reaction provided substrate (H2O2) for Cu ion enzyme-like reaction. This double enzyme cascade reaction promoted each other and continuously provided each other with reaction substrate, forming a virtuous cycle. The results of fluorescence quantification showed that oxygen production was greater than oxygen consumption.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigated levels of H2O2, GSH and ROS
ce:section.ce:para: H2O2 detection kit was used to investigate the level of H2O2 in cells. HA@GOx, HA@CuFACDs-TPP and HCFCTG were co-incubated with CT26 cells respectively, a blank control group was set up, and operations were performed according to kit instructions. The results showed the level of H2O2 in cells as shown in Fig. 5a. Compared with the control group, H2O2 level in the HA@GOx group was up-regulated by 120 %, the HA@CuFACDs-TPP group was down-regulated by 60 %, and the HCFCTG group was up-regulated by 60 %. In the cell, HA@CuFACDs-TPP Fenton reaction consumes H2O2, resulting in a decrease in H2O2 concentration. HA@GOx decomposes glucose to produce H2O2, which supplemented the intracellular H2O2 concentration, resulting in increased H2O2 levels, demonstrated the ability of GOx to increase H2O2. In HCFCTG cells, both Fenton-like reaction consumes H2O2 and glucose oxidation generates H2O2. These two processes are in competition with each other. Judging from the results, H2O2 level increased during incubation, indicating that the reaction rate of GOx enzyme was higher than that of peroxidase under the premise of sufficient glucose. The excellent H2O2 self-supply capability of HCFCTG was demonstrated.

GSH is an important obstacle to CDT therapy, and tumor cells secrete large amounts of GSH to consume ROS in response to oxidative stress [57], in particular, GSH is more sensitive to exogenous ROS. The GSH assay kit was used to investigate the GSH levels in CT26 cells. The results were shown in Fig. 5b, increasing the concentration of HCFCTG can significantly reduce the level of GSH, indicating that HCFCTG had a strong ability to deplete GSH, which was further enhance with the aid of 808 nm laser photothermal effect. At high doses (400 μg mL−1), GSH levels were reduced by 72 % in the no-light group and 83 % in the light group.

DCFH-DA can be deacetylated by lactase to non-fluorescein and oxidized by ROS to green fluorescein 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescein (DCF), which is commonly used as intracellular ROS probe [58]. As can be seen from Fig. 5c, d, no fluorescence was detected in the cells of the control group, while the cells treated with HA@CuFACDs-TPP and HCFCTG showed bright and strong green fluorescence, indicating that peroxidase-like reaction produced ⋅OH. At the same dose (based on the concentration of CuFACDs), the fluorescence of HCFCTG group was stronger than that of HA@CuFACDs-TPP group, indicating that the former had more ⋅OH production, which was attributed to the contribution of GOx, which triggered the glucose oxidation reaction to provide more substrates for the peroxisase-like reaction, and produced more ⋅OH. The effect of photothermal effect on ROS was investigated. As expected, more ⋅OH was produced under 808 nm laser irradiation, and the warming effect enhanced peroxidase like reaction. At high dose (400 μg mL−1), ROS levels in the light group were 19 % higher than those in the no light group. It can be seen that GOx and temperature contribute significantly to enhancing Fenton-like reaction.

The toxicity of GOx, CuFACDs-TPP and HCFCTG to CT26 cells was evaluated by MTT assay. The concentration gradient of HCFCTG was 0, 50, 100, 200 and 400 μg mL−1. The concentration gradient of GOx calculated according to drug loading rate was 0, 4.6, 9.2, 18.4, 36.8 μg mL−1. The concentration gradient of CuFACDs-TPP was 0, 15.9, 31.7, 63.4 and 126.8 μg mL−1, respectively. As shown in Fig. 5e, GOx, CuFACDs-TPP, and HCFCTG all shown cytotoxicity. GOx triggered glucose oxidation reaction to deplete glucose, cut off the energy supply of tumor cells, starved cancer cells by starvation therapy (ST). The GOx group had only a single ST effect. The GOx group shown dose-dependent inhibition with a maximum inhibition rate of 25 % (in 36.8 μg mL−1 group). CuFACDs-TPP dark group shown dose-dependent inhibition of tumor cell growth. CuFACDs-TPP group had only CDT effects. The inhibition rate of CuFACDs-TPP dark group was 39 % (126.8 μg mL−1). At the same dose, the inhibition rate of CuFACDs-TPP light group was 46 %, indicating that the photothermal assisted effect enhanced CDT (Increased by 7 %). HCFCTG group had both ST and CDT, and photothermal assisted effect under light condition. The cell inhibition rate of HCFCTG (400 μg mL−1) was 96 % in the dark group and 100 % in the light group. In summary, the cell inhibition rate of HCFCTG group, GOx group and CuFACDs-TPP group were 96 %, 25 % and 39 %, respectively, and the inhibition rate of HCFCTG was greater than the sum of the latter two groups, and the therapeutic effect of “1 + 1 > 2” was obtained, which proved that the synergistic therapeutic effect of CDT/ST was significant. Moreover, the inhibition rate of cell viability was further improved with the help of photothermal effect of 808 nm laser.

In order to investigate the cytotoxicity more directly, the distribution of live and dead cells was evaluated by double staining of live and dead cells [59]. CT26 cells were treated with different samples and then stained with Calcein acetoxymethyl ester (Calcein-AM) and propidium iodide (PI). Living and dead cells appear green and red, respectively. As shown in Fig. 5f and g, the cells treated without drugs grew well, while the cells treated with HA@GOx had weak damage, indicating that ST therapy had occurred, but ST therapy could only kill a small part of the tumor cells at the dose in this study. The number of dead cells increased significantly after treatment with HA@CuFACDs-TPP, which showed a strong CDT therapeutic effect. It was worth noting that more cells died after treatment with HCFCTG, which is due to the CDT/ST synergistic therapy enhanced by self-supplied H2O2 and O2. It should also be noted that more cells died in the light group than in the dark group, especially almost all cells died in the HCFCTG light group, highlighting the advantages of the synergistic treatment of PTT, ST and CDT.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Imaging and drug distribution in vivo
ce:section.ce:para: The excellent photothermal properties, fluorescence imaging properties and high efficiency in vitro treatment of HCFCTG have prompted us to further explore the feasibility of in vivo tumor treatment. Accurate localization and determination of the timing of accumulation are particularly important for successful treatment of solid tumors. Therefore, after injecting HCFCTG into the tail vein, near-infrared photothermal imaging and fluorescence imaging were performed on CT26 colorectal cancer tumor model mice to investigate the biological distribution of HCFCTG in vivo in order to optimize the therapeutic window of HCFCTG. Balb/c CT26 cells tumor-bearing mice were the model mice, and the fluorescence intensity at the tumor site was observed in the small animal living imager after tail vein administration. Mouse colon cancer cell line CT26 cells were subcutaneously inoculated on the right back of mice, and HCFCTG was injected through the tail vein at the dose of 10 mg kg−1 when the tumor volume reached 100 mm−3. As shown in Fig. 6a, the fluorescence signal in the tumor was gradually enhanced, reaching a peak at 6 h after injection, and drugs were still enriched at the tumor site 12 h later. After 24 h, there were still drug residues in the tumor, it indicated that the drug has a longer retention period in the body. Fig. 6b was the result of photothermal imaging in vivo of mice after administration, and it was found that it was significantly enriched at the tumor site at 4 h after administration, reached a peak at 6 h, and there was still a large amount of drug residue after 12 h. At 6 h after administration, major organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung and kidney) and solid tumors of mice were taken and photographed on the fluorescence imager. The results were shown in Fig. 6c. The fluorescence was strongest in tumor tissue, followed by liver and kidney, confirming that drug accumulation was the most in tumor, followed by liver and kidney. Fig. 6d, e and f was fluorescence imaging, photothermal imaging and quantitative fluorescence histograms of solid tumors in mice, respectively. In vivo fluorescence imaging and photothermal imaging experiments confirmed that HCFCTG targeted enrichment at the tumor site and reached its peak 6 h after administration. Therefore, it was determined that 6 h after administration is the best timing for light irradiation therapy.

7-week-old female Balb/c mice were used as model animals. CT26 cells were inoculated subcutaneously, and when the tumor volume was about 100 mm3, they were randomly divided into 5 groups (5 mice per group): Saline group, GOx group, CuFACDs-TPP group, GOx + CuFACDs-TPP group and HCFCTG+Laser group. According to the treatment plan (Fig. 7a), drug was administered on day 0, day 5, and day 10, light irradiation was performed 6 h after drug administration, and treatment was ended on day 14. HCFCTG was administered through the tail vein at a dose of 10 mg kg−1 and irradiated with 808 nm laser for 10 min. Fig. 7b shown the imaging of solid tumors after 14 days of treatment. Fig. 7c shown the curve of tumor volume change during treatment. It is found that tumors in Saline group grew rapidly without restriction, and tumor volume increase 10.6 times within 14 days. The solid tumor volume in the GOx group was 77 % of that in the control group, and the tumor growth inhibition rate is 23 %, indicating that the consumption of glucose oxidase reduces the energy supply, and the rapid tumor growth trend was inhibited. The solid tumor volume in CuFACDs-TPP group was significantly lower than that in GOx group, and the growth inhibition rate was 44 %, indicating that CDT treatment alone significantly slow down the growth rate of tumor at the dose in this study; The tumor growth inhibition rate of GOx + CuFACDs-TPP group was 75 %, which was significantly higher than that of CuFACDs-TPP group and GOx group, demonstrating the synergistic therapeutic effect (1 + 1 > 2), confirming that the synergistic effect of starvation therapy and chemodynamic therapy amplified the therapeutic effect. This was due to the fact that GOx provides more H2O2 for Fenton-like reaction, thus producing more ROS, and thus enhancing the anti-tumor effect. The HCFCTG+Laser group had the smallest solid tumor and the greatest restriction on tumor growth, with an inhibition rate of >98 %, and tumor growth was almost completely inhibited. The anti-tumor effect was significantly stronger than that of GOx + CuFACDs-TPP group, which was attribute to the photothermal effect of 808 nm laser, which converted light energy into heat energy. High temperature promoted the Fenton-like reaction of CuFACDs-TPP, which enhanced ST and CDT. In conclusion, the synergistic effect of the HCFCTG+Laser group based on CDT/ST effectively ablated solid tumors.

Fig. 7e shown the H&E staining results of major organs and tumor tissues after treatment. It is found that there are no traces of damage in heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney tissues, The tumor tissue in the treatment group had different degrees of injury, and the degree of injury was aggravated according to the order of GOx group, CuFACDs-TPP group, GOx + CuFACDs-TPP group, and HCFCTG+Light group. Tumor damage was most severe in HCFCTG+Light group, demonstrating the advantage of HCFCTG based on ST/CDT/PTT synergistic effect.

In cell experiments, we have confirmed that HCFCTG has obvious self‑oxygenating effect. Therefore, we used immunofluorescence to investigate the concentration level of HIF-1α, a marker of hypoxia in tumor tissues after treatment, and the results were shown in Fig. 7f. After staining, HIF-1α produces green fluorescence, and the fluorescence intensity was proportional to the its concentration. The green fluorescence intensity of the GOx group was higher than that of the saline group, indicating that the concentration of HIF-1α was up-regulated. The reason was that GOx consumes oxygen in the process of oxidizing glucose, aggravated tumor hypoxia, and induced HIF-1α upregulation. The fluorescence intensity of the CuFACDs-TPP group was lower than that of the control group, indicating that HIF-1α was down-regulated, confirming that the O2 concentration was up-regulated based on Fenton-like reaction. The green fluorescence intensity of the GOx + CuFACDs-TPP group was between the CuFACDs-TPP group and the GOx group, which was due to the existence of the oxygen generation process of Fenton-like reaction and the oxygen consumption process of GOx at the same time. As a result of the competition between these two processes, the oxygen generation process dominated, thus increasing the O2 level and then lowers HIF-1α. The HCFCTG+Laser group had the lowest fluorescence intensity, indicating the lowest HIF-1α level, which was attributed to the photothermal effect which further amplified the O2 level. In conclusion, HCFCTG increased O2 concentration and down-regulate HIF-1α with the assistance of laser, which was conducive to consolidating the therapeutic effect of CDT.

In summary, we have developed a novel multifunctional nanodrug delivery system HCFCTG. HCFCTG promotes the double enzyme cascade by increasing O2 and H2O2 in situ under the stimulation of tumor microenvironment. HCFCTG is used to solve the problem of hydrogen peroxide deficiency and tumor hypoxia in CDT therapy. The HCFCTG is preferentially enriched at tumor sites under the mediation of HA, and pH stimulate the released of peroxide-like nanozymes (CuFACDs-TPP) and GOx. Base on the collaboration of ST-mediated energy cut-off with the peroxide-like activity of CuFACDs-TPP, HCFCTG can effectively starve tumor cells and produce ROS, causing tumor cell death. The photothermal effect of 808 nm laser enhances the double enzyme cascade reaction and amplifies the treatment effect. Both in vitro and in vivo experiments have demonstrated that HCFCTG has significant anti-tumor effects. HCFCTG has negligible side effects on normal tissues/organs, demonstrating good biocompatibility. Overall, this work shows that starvation therapy/chemokinetic therapy/photothermal therapy can be successfully integrated into one, providing a synergistic therapeutic strategy for more effective and safer tumor treatment.

Zhiqiang Wang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Xiaodan Wu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Yingxue Jin: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.
